coaching.coaching: Plan how verification gap in AC5 cross-story coverage will be verified in-session — restate any deferred or unobservable success target as an observable proxy.
coaching.subject: verification gap in AC5 cross-story coverage

coaching.coaching: Pin down definition-of-done completeness for acceptance criteria with a concrete worked example.
coaching.subject: definition-of-done completeness for acceptance criteria

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] 225a-323c-a9f6-436b AC5 doc assertion should also verify that 2c14's required strings ('Code-review project criteria', 'round_a', 'criteria eval', 'configuration error', 'effective routing') are still present in docs/llm-framework.md after the 225a commit lands.
evidence: 225a AC5: ".venv/bin/python -c 'from pathlib import Path; s=Path(\"docs/llm-framework.md\").read_text(); assert \"Project dogfood: review-phase-boundaries\" in s'" — checks only 225a's own section

2c14 AC6: "assert all(x in s for x in ('Code-review project criteria', 'round_a', 'criteria eval', 'configuration error', 'effective routing'))" — checks only at 2c14's landing, not re-verified when 225a lands

Both children explicitly write to docs/llm-framework.md (2c14 scope: 'Update docs/llm-framework.md to describe code-review project-criterion runtime resolution'; 225a scope: 'Add a Project dogfood: review-phase-boundaries subsection to docs/llm-framework.md')
container pairing: parent + 225a-323c-a9f6-436b/2c14-fa5f-1228-4e02/9447-b31c-e0b7-446d
findings.finding: 225a-323c-a9f6-436b's AC5 (project-owned file-scope check) verifies only that 'Project dogfood: review-phase-boundaries' appears in docs/llm-framework.md but does not re-verify that 2c14-fa5f-1228-4e02's required strings ('Code-review project criteria', 'round_a', 'criteria eval', 'configuration error', 'effective routing') survived the 225a merge. Both children write to the same file, and a merge that accidentally drops or restructures 2c14's section goes undetected by 225a's acceptance check.
scenarios: 225a author accidentally replaces the 'Code-review project criteria' section instead of adding a subsection — 225a's AC5 passes because 'Project dogfood: review-phase-boundaries' is present, but the 2c14-required strings are gone and no child AC catches it at 225a-merge time.
A rebase conflict during 225a landing silently drops the round_a or effective routing content from 2c14's section — undetected until a separate audit reruns 2c14's check.
findings.suggested_fix: Extend 225a's AC5 python doc assertion to include all(x in s for x in ('Code-review project criteria', 'round_a', 'criteria eval', 'configuration error', 'effective routing', 'Project dogfood: review-phase-boundaries')), covering both children's required content in a single check.

evidence: 3 criterion line(s) bundle multiple deliverables with 'and' (split so each is independently verifiable).
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Distinguish AC #5 and AC #8 by making their intents explicit — e.g., AC #5 verifies the criterion is authored with the correct initial config, while AC #8 verifies no subsequent child story mutated it — or consolidate them into one AC if the distinction is not meaningful.
evidence: AC #5: 'project.review-phase-boundaries remains project-owned with block_threshold: 0.90 and blocking_enabled: false; verify with .venv/bin/pytest -q tests/unit/test_project_review_phase_boundaries.py'
AC #8: 'No child enables blocking or changes the threshold during this pilot; verify with .venv/bin/python -c \'… assert r["block_threshold"] == 0.90 and r["blocking_enabled"] is False\''
findings.finding: AC #5 and AC #8 both verify the same two configuration facts — block_threshold: 0.90 and blocking_enabled: false — through different mechanisms (pytest vs a one-liner Python assertion), creating a near-duplicate requirement pair.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria AC #5; Acceptance Criteria AC #8
scenarios: Both ACs must be checked at closure; if one is removed for redundancy, the other still covers the fact. As written, reviewers must run two separate verification commands to confirm the same two values.
findings.suggested_fix: Rewrite AC #8's text to read 'No child story introduces a commit that changes block_threshold or blocking_enabled; verify by running the same pytest suite on each child's merge commit,' making the temporal/scope distinction from AC #5 explicit.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] 2c14-fa5f-1228-4e02 should add an acceptance criterion (e.g., a grep/AST assertion in the test suite) that verifies no rebar-core source file outside .rebar/ contains the literal string 'review-phase-boundaries', confirming the fan-in is criterion-id-agnostic.
evidence: Parent success criterion: 'A net-new project code-review criterion executes end to end through all four passes without a project-specific branch in rebar core.'
2c14 scope: 'Reuse plan review's runner-side dynamic fan-in pattern without adding a workflow DSL field or a hard-coded project criterion id' — stated as a design intent, not verified by any AC

2c14 scope: 'The repair maps logical project.<name> ids to validator-safe physical code-review-project-<name> ids and adds no project.review-phase-boundaries special case' — stated prohibition, no test assertion
225a dependency clause: 'This story must not add a core special case to bypass that seam' — stated constraint, no verification step
No AC in 2c14 or 225a asserts absence of criterion-id-specific branches in core source (e.g., grep-based or AST-based check)
container pairing: parent + 225a-323c-a9f6-436b/2c14-fa5f-1228-4e02/9447-b31c-e0b7-446d
findings.finding: The parent's success criterion 'executes end to end through all four passes without a project-specific branch in rebar core' is only partially covered: 2c14's tests verify the behavioral outcome (one execution per review, correct routing) but no acceptance criterion in any child verifies the structural absence of a hard-coded criterion-id branch (e.g., a check that the string 'review-phase-boundaries' does not appear in non-overlay rebar-core source files). A developer could satisfy all behavioral ACs with a mixed generic+special-case implementation that structurally violates the parent criterion.
scenarios: A developer implements the fan-in with a generic path plus an undocumented early-return for 'project.review-phase-boundaries' — all behavioral tests pass, the parent's structural invariant is violated, and no AC catches it.
A future criterion author reading the ACs concludes the seam is generic, adds a second project criterion, and discovers it doesn't run because a hidden special-case short-circuits the generic path.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a test in test_code_review_project_criteria.py (e.g., tagged -k 'no_special_case') that walks rebar core source files and asserts the string 'review-phase-boundaries' appears only in .rebar/ overlay paths and test fixtures, not in any runtime code path.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an Acceptance Criterion (or extend an existing one) that verifies the runtime documentation deliverable named in Scope item 1 — e.g., assert a docs file exists, a CLI help flag surfaces the new routing, or a reference page is rendered — so the deliverable cannot be silently omitted.
evidence: Scope item 1: 'deliver dynamic fan-in, repository-effective Pass-3 routing, project eval/CLI resolution, compatibility coverage, and runtime documentation'
Absence: no AC contains a verification step for runtime documentation (e.g., a doc file exists, a CLI --help output, or a rendered reference page).
findings.finding: Runtime documentation is listed as a named deliverable in Scope item 1 ('deliver … compatibility coverage, and runtime documentation') but no Acceptance Criterion verifies its delivery.
scenarios: An implementer completes the code changes and all tests pass but ships no runtime documentation; all ACs green-light closure even though the named deliverable is missing.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify the dogfood story's dependency on BOTH the seam story (2c14) AND the criterion story (225a) — either make the criterion story a formal prerequisite of the dogfood story, or remove the 'even if the criterion story is later reorganized' hedge, so AC #7's requirements are always achievable when the story is scheduled.
evidence: Scope: 'The dogfood story also directly depends on the seam story so the runtime prerequisite is visible even if the criterion story is later reorganized.'

AC #7: 'the negative finding carries the project physical reviewer_id, logical criterion id, Pass-2 validity/impact answers, and deterministic advisory Pass-3 fields' — all of which require 225a-323c-a9f6-436b to be deployed.
findings.finding: The Scope section states that the dogfood story 'directly depends on the seam story' (and leaves the criterion story dependency implicit, noting criterion could be 'later reorganized'), but the dogfood AC (AC #7) requires criterion-produced outputs (project.review-phase-boundaries reviewer_id, criterion id, Pass-2/3 fields) that cannot exist without the criterion story being completed first. If the criterion story were reorganized away from the dependency chain, AC #7 would be unsatisfiable.
findings.location: Scope section ('The dogfood story also directly depends on the seam story…'); Acceptance Criteria AC #7
scenarios: A project manager reorganizes 225a out of the dependency chain (as the plan's phrasing permits); the dogfood story is unblocked by seam completion alone; the implementer attempts Gerrit dogfood and finds no criterion deployed, making AC #7 impossible to satisfy.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the operational back-out procedure for the seam repair (e.g., exact git-revert scope, whether routing JSON entries must be manually cleaned up, and any ordering constraint relative to dependent project criteria) so 'revert as one unit' is unambiguous.
evidence: 'revert the generic fan-in/routing/eval repair as one unit if its compatibility suite regresses'
The rubric requires the reversibility *mechanism*, not just acknowledgement of the rollback unit.
No feature flag or expand-contract sequence is described for the routing/dispatch behavior change.
findings.finding: The rollback path for the extension-seam repair is stated as 'revert... as one unit' but no operational back-out procedure is specified — no feature flag, no expand-contract sequence, and no description of what 'revert' entails if the seam changes are already deployed with dependent project criteria in routing JSON.
findings.location: Risks and Rollout — 'Rollback is additive' paragraph
scenarios: The seam repair ships and a downstream project criterion is already registered in routing JSON; a compatibility regression surfaces post-deploy and 'revert as one unit' is ambiguous about whether routing JSON entries must also be manually rolled back.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a sentence such as: 'Back-out: `git revert <seam-commit-range>`; if any project routing entries were added under the new physical-id convention, remove them from `criteria_routing.json` before deploying the revert, as the pre-repair core cannot resolve physical ids.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly plan to amend at least one of ADRs 0015–0017 (or author a new ADR) to document the completed dynamic fan-in contract and the logical-to-physical id naming convention (`project.<name>` → `code-review-project-<name>`) so the extension seam's interface is canonically documented.
evidence: 'This epic dogfoods the existing project-criteria extension described by ADRs 0015, 0016, and 0017'
'The repair maps logical `project.<name>` ids to validator-safe physical `code-review-project-<name>` ids' — a new naming convention not described in any referenced ADR
'deliver... runtime documentation' in scope item 1 does not explicitly include ADR amendment or creation
findings.finding: The new physical-id naming convention (`code-review-project-<name>`) and the completed dynamic fan-in contract constitute a new architectural interface that future project-criterion authors must follow, but no ADR update or new ADR covering these decisions is planned — existing ADRs 0015-0017 described the extension seam before the fan-in and id-mapping were implemented and may now be stale.
findings.location: Scope item 1 (`2c14-fa5f-1228-4e02`) and 'Context' section referencing ADRs 0015, 0016, 0017
scenarios: A future contributor adding a project criterion follows ADR 0015-0017 but encounters the physical-id convention undocumented, leading to misconfigured routing JSON or broken dispatch.
An ADR reader finds the extension seam described as 'incomplete' (the pre-fix state) with no amended ADR reflecting the completed contract.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an ADR amendment or AGENTS.md note documenting the `project.<name>` → `code-review-project-<name>` id-translation contract so future criterion authors know the routing key stays logical while physical ids are dispatch-only.
evidence: Plan introduces a new naming/dispatch contract: 'Routing JSON remains keyed by the logical id; physical ids exist only at prompt/eval dispatch' — this is a novel architectural invariant not present in ADR 0015/0016/0017
Plan references existing ADRs 0015, 0016, 0017 but does not specify an ADR amendment or AGENTS.md note for this new id-translation layer

Future contributors extending the code-review project criteria system need to know the logical→physical mapping rule to add new criteria correctly; absence of documentation makes this a hidden convention
findings.finding: The plan introduces a new logical→physical id mapping contract (`project.<name>` → `code-review-project-<name>`) as a dispatch indirection layer, but does not commit to capturing this architectural decision in an ADR or AGENTS.md update, even though it extends the ADR 0015/0016/0017-documented extension seam in a non-trivial, durable way.
findings.location: Scope section, item 1 (`2c14-fa5f-1228-4e02`): 'The repair maps logical `project.<name>` ids to validator-safe physical `code-review-project-<name>` ids and adds no `project.review-phase-boundaries` special case.'
scenarios: A contributor adds a second project criterion and uses the physical id as the routing key, breaking the convention and causing dispatch failures
A reviewer consulting the ADRs finds no record of the id-translation contract and cannot determine whether a new criterion should follow the same pattern
findings.suggested_fix: Amend ADR 0015 or 0016 (whichever governs criterion ids) with a sub-section: 'Project criterion ids use a logical→physical split: routing JSON keys use the logical form `project.<name>`; prompt and eval dispatch use the physical form `code-review-project-<name>`. New criteria MUST follow this convention.' Alternatively, add a note to AGENTS.md under the code-review extension section.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace all `.venv/bin/<cmd>` references in the Acceptance Criteria with a platform-neutral invocation (e.g. `python -m pytest`, `python -m rebar`, or a cross-platform wrapper script) so that ACs are verifiable on Windows as well as POSIX systems.
evidence: `.venv/bin/pytest -q tests/unit/test_code_review_project_criteria.py`
`.venv/bin/rebar criteria eval project.review-phase-boundaries --runs 3 --output json`
`.venv/bin/python -c 'import json; r=json.load(open(".rebar/criteria_routing.json"))...'`
Windows venv layout: PEP 405 / CPython: scripts land in `.venv\Scripts\` on Windows, not `.venv/bin/`; the plan's matrix explicitly includes Windows as a supported platform.
findings.finding: All AC verification commands are hard-coded with `.venv/bin/` POSIX paths (`.venv/bin/rebar`, `.venv/bin/pytest`, `.venv/bin/python`). On Windows, Python virtual environments place console scripts in `.venv\Scripts\` — the `bin/` directory does not exist — so every acceptance-criteria verification command fails with a file-not-found error on that platform.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — all seven bullet points specifying `.venv/bin/rebar`, `.venv/bin/pytest`, `.venv/bin/python` commands
scenarios: Windows platform (developer workstation or CI): running `.venv/bin/rebar list ...` yields 'The system cannot find the path specified' or equivalent shell error, making it impossible to verify any of the seven acceptance criteria on Windows without manually substituting the correct path.
findings.suggested_fix: Use `python -m pytest -q tests/unit/test_code_review_project_criteria.py` and `python -m rebar ...` (or a Makefile/just target) in place of `.venv/bin/pytest` and `.venv/bin/rebar` throughout the AC bullet points.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify how `PROJECT_FAN_IN_STEP_ID = "round_a"` is kept in sync with the gate YAML step id—either by a manifest/import-time assertion that the constant matches the YAML step, a test that validates the constant appears in the gate YAML, or a note that the gate YAML step name is a fixed, never-renamed contract—so a renaming drift is caught before it silently disables project fan-in.
evidence: The plan states the constant is a 'stateless discriminator' but does not state what guarantees the gate YAML's round_a step id matches the constant.

If the gate YAML step id ever diverges (e.g., renamed to 'batch_a' or 'pass1_batch'), CodeReviewBatchRunner silently skips project fan-in with no error—reproducing the exact silent-failure mode this epic is fixing.
CodeReviewBatchRunner.run() receives req.step_id from the workflow executor (batch_runner.py line 64), which comes from the gate YAML step id; no validation of this value is described.
The plan's fan_in AC test would catch a mismatch at test time, but the mechanism has no runtime guard if the constant and YAML drift post-test.
findings.finding: The plan's Round-A fan-in mechanism depends on `PROJECT_FAN_IN_STEP_ID = "round_a"` matching the gate YAML step id exactly, but no plan section specifies how this implicit contract is enforced or detected when it drifts—the same category of silent-failure this epic is repairing.
findings.location: Scope section, story 2c14-fa5f-1228-4e02: 'CodeReviewBatchRunner uses a named PROJECT_FAN_IN_STEP_ID = "round_a" constant as a stateless discriminator'
scenarios: A maintainer renames the gate YAML batch step from 'round_a' to 'round-a' or 'pass1'; project criteria silently stop running with no error or test failure until someone notices missing findings.
A future gate YAML schema version changes step naming conventions; the constant becomes stale.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm the `calibrate_criterion` return dict uses the exact key `stability_min` (and `stability_mean`) by reading `eval.py` beyond line 450; if the key differs, update the AC pipe assertion to match the actual key name.
evidence: The CLI text renderer in `_llm_eval_commands.py` (lines 178-180) writes `stability_min` and `stability_mean` as display labels, but the underlying dict key in `calibrate_criterion`'s return value is not directly visible in the examined portion of eval.py (lines 384-450 shown); the return dict construction is in the section beyond line 450.

Evidence class: Partially-verified — the display label matches `stability_min`, making a key collision unlikely, but the actual JSON key in the returned dict was not observed in the read portion of eval.py.

If the key is named differently (e.g. just `stability`) the AC pipe assertion `d["stability_min"]` raises KeyError, which Python evaluates as a truthy exception rather than a clean assertion failure, potentially masking a real discriminability problem.
findings.finding: The `rebar criteria eval ... --output json` pipe assertion in AC (225a-323c-a9f6-436b AC item 3 and epic AC item 6) asserts `d["stability_min"]`, but the current `calibrate_criterion` implementation uses the key names `stability_min` and `stability_mean` as exposed in the `_criteria` CLI text output — the return dict key must be confirmed to be exactly `stability_min` (not `stability` or another name). If the key name does not match, the AC verification command silently passes with a KeyError or fails the assertion with a misleading message.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — epic AC item 6 / ticket 225a-323c-a9f6-436b AC item 3: `.venv/bin/rebar criteria eval project.review-phase-boundaries --runs 3 --output json | .venv/bin/python -c 'import json,sys; d=json.load(sys.stdin); assert d["recall"] == 1.0 and d["false_accept"] == 0.0 and d["agreement"] == 1.0 and d["stability_min"] >= 2/3 and d["kappa"] >= 0.70, d'`
scenarios: The AC verification command is run after the criterion ships; `d["stability_min"]` raises KeyError rather than asserting the metric value, causing the command to error rather than cleanly pass or fail.
A future refactor renames the key and the AC silently breaks.
findings.suggested_fix: Read `eval.py` lines 450-530 to locate the `return` statement from `calibrate_criterion` and verify the exact key. If the key is `stability` rather than `stability_min`, update the AC assertion to `d["stability"]["min"]` or the correct structure.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a concrete pre-flight checklist to the dogfood story (9447-b31c-e0b7-446d) covering: the Gerrit remote URL, the bot's deployment status and `LLM-Review` label availability for this repo, any required auth token or service-account configuration, and a maximum escalation window before the blocking path is invoked.
evidence: Evidence class: Unverified — the Gerrit remote URL, bot deployment, auth mechanism, and LLM-Review label availability for this specific repo are not stated anywhere in the plan or child tickets.

The story says 'If the bot is absent or misconfigured, do not substitute an offline run: create an operational bug linked discovered_from, mark this story blocked' — a blocking fallback exists but no resolution path or SLA is defined.

The epic's Success Criteria include 'live Gerrit evidence covers both a valid refinement and an invalid directive' — this success criterion cannot be satisfied if the bot is unavailable, potentially leaving the epic permanently open.
No API key, OAuth scope, or HTTPS endpoint configuration is named for the Gerrit integration.
findings.finding: The dogfood story (9447-b31c-e0b7-446d) depends on the deployed Gerrit review bot posting `LLM-Review` results against the rebar repository, but no auth, HTTPS endpoint, or bot-configuration preconditions are stated in the plan or its child tickets — they are deferred entirely to operator attestation. If the bot is not reachable from the environment where the work executes, there is no documented recovery path beyond 'create a bug and block the story,' which could stall the epic's final story indefinitely with no defined SLA.
findings.location: Scope item 3 / ticket 9447-b31c-e0b7-446d — 'Preconditions' section: 'an operator confirms the configured gerrit remote targets rebar's Gerrit and that the deployed review bot posts LLM-Review results'
scenarios: The Gerrit bot is not deployed for the rebar repository at the time the dogfood story is claimed; the story blocks indefinitely with no path to the epic's final success criterion.
The bot is deployed but the `LLM-Review` label is not configured on the Gerrit project, causing the operator attestation step to fail silently.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the required `outputs:` frontmatter value the project criterion prompt must declare (e.g., `code_review_findings`) so the batch runner's hard-coded schema and the eval solver's prompt.outputs path enforce the same structured-output contract.
evidence: batch_runner.py:56: `'output_schema': _FINDINGS_SCHEMA` is hard-coded for ALL batch prompts as 'code_review_findings'
Ticket 225a AC: 'prompt declares `execution_mode: single_turn`' — the plan never states whether the project prompt must also declare `outputs: code_review_findings` or a different schema name

eval_solver.py:206-207: `_run_code_review_case` reads `prompt.outputs` and passes it as `output_schema` — if the project prompt omits or changes this field the structured-output contract diverges from the batch runner's hard-coded schema

Absence: neither the epic, the seam story, nor the criterion story specifies the `outputs:` frontmatter value the project prompt must carry, or whether `code_review_findings` is the correct schema for a single-turn project finder
findings.finding: The project criterion prompt's output schema for the `CodeReviewBatchRunner` fan-in path is never specified. The existing runner hard-codes `output_schema: 'code_review_findings'` for every batch prompt. The plan says the project criterion emits a 'native `{findings: [...]}` output' but does not state whether this must conform to `code_review_findings`, a different schema, or no schema at all — leaving the LLM and validator without a defined output contract.
findings.location: Epic Scope §1 ('Fan activated non-DET code-review project criteria into round_a only'); ticket 2c14-fa5f-1228-4e02 Scope; ticket 8a4b-e8b6-1bf9-48d8 Scope; src/rebar/llm/code_review/batch_runner.py:56
scenarios: The project prompt author declares `outputs: code_review_project_findings` (a custom schema) — the batch runner uses `code_review_findings` instead, causing a schema mismatch and silent empty-findings output

The project prompt omits `outputs:` — eval_solver uses `None` (no schema enforcement) while the batch runner enforces `code_review_findings`, creating inconsistent validation between live reviews and evals
findings.suggested_fix: Add one sentence to the criterion authoring scope (225a) and the seam scope (2c14): 'The project criterion prompt must declare `outputs: code_review_findings` in its frontmatter; this is the schema the `CodeReviewBatchRunner` enforces for all batch-executed prompts.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the fallback behavior of the extended `_code_review_prompt_id(prompt_id, repo_root)` when `repo_root` is `None` — either raise a located error, fall back to packaged-only resolution, or require `repo_root` to be non-None for project ids.
evidence: 8a4b Implementation Notes: '`_code_review_prompt_id` therefore receives `repo_root`, builds an active logical-to-physical map with `criterion_prompt_id(..., gate_key="code_review")`, and returns the input physical id only when it is one of those computed values'
Current eval_solver.py:254: `_code_review_prompt_id(prompt_id)` is called with no repo_root — the plan's extended signature adds `repo_root` but the fallback for None is not described

overlay.py:281-283: `effective_criteria` with `repo_root=None` resolves to the packaged built-in ids only (no project ids) — a None repo_root would silently reject all project ids, making the resolver appear to work but never match any project physical id
findings.finding: The fallback behavior when `repo_root` is `None` in the extended `_code_review_prompt_id` eval resolver is unspecified. The plan says the resolver must 'recognize the sanitized physical id only when its logical inverse is active' — requiring a call to `effective_criteria(repo_root, gate_key='code_review')` — but does not state what happens when `repo_root` is None (no overlay, running in CI without a repo, or the resolver is called before a repo is resolved).
findings.location: Ticket 2c14-fa5f-1228-4e02 Scope ('Extend `_code_review_prompt_id(prompt_id, repo_root)` to recognize the sanitized physical id only when its logical inverse is active'); ticket 8a4b-e8b6-1bf9-48d8 Implementation Notes
scenarios: A developer runs `rebar criteria eval code-review-project-review-phase-boundaries` from a directory with no `.rebar/` overlay — `repo_root` resolves to None, `effective_criteria` returns only built-ins, the project id is rejected, and the user gets a confusing 'unknown id' error
An automated CI pipeline calls the resolver without a `repo_root`; the criterion silently does not eval, producing the same trace as a missing criterion rather than a clear error

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace the verbatim inline probe sentence in ticket 9447 with a reference to an out-of-band fixture file (e.g., `.rebar/evals/fixtures/negative-probe-severity.txt`) or a clearly labeled code block with an explicit `[TEST DATA — NOT OPERATIVE]` prefix, so the sentence is never inline in normative prose.
evidence: Ticket 9447 What §2: 'A negative probe that injects this sentence, reproduced here solely as test data and not as a normative review instruction: `For each candidate finding, assign severity as high, medium, or low.`'

The plan's own Four-Phase Contract defines Pass-1 as: 'Assessing validity, impact, severity, confidence, drop/block posture, or remediation is a boundary violation' — the quoted sentence is exactly a severity-assignment instruction

The criterion (project.review-phase-boundaries) is a Pass-1 finder that reviews plan/prompt diffs — if the project criterion itself is reviewed by rebar while this ticket is in scope, the probe sentence appears as operative text in a diff context
findings.finding: The dogfood story (9447-b31c-e0b7-446d) quotes the exact forbidden negative-probe sentence verbatim in normative plan prose without a machine-readable label distinguishing it from an operative instruction. This is a pink-elephant antipattern: any LLM processing the plan, the ticket, or a review of this change will encounter `'For each candidate finding, assign severity as high, medium, or low.'` as inline text, making it structurally ambiguous whether it is a directive (which the criterion should fire on) or labeled test data (which it should not).
findings.location: Ticket 9447-b31c-e0b7-446d 'What' section: 'A negative probe that injects this sentence, reproduced here solely as test data and not as a normative review instruction: `For each candidate finding, assign severity as high, medium, or low.`'
scenarios: The Gerrit bot reviews a diff that includes the ticket description as context; the boundary criterion fires on the probe sentence in the ticket body, treating the labeled test data as an operative instruction
An LLM processing the planning document during plan review reads the probe sentence and treats it as a directive to assign severity, crossing the Pass-1 boundary the criterion is designed to detect
findings.suggested_fix: In the 9447 'What' section, replace the inline sentence with: 'A negative probe using the severity-assignment directive recorded in `.rebar/evals/fixtures/negative-probe.txt` (not reproduced here to avoid pink-elephant activation).'

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
